author: waychane
summary: 《燃比娃》是由李文愉执导、上海电影(集团)有限公司及上海美术电影制片厂有限公司出品的动画电影，也是国内首部宣纸手绘动画长片。本片导演李文愉毕业于北京大学、硕士研究生学历，毕业后任教于四川大学艺术学院数
content:
用 iPad「画」出来的电影：《燃比娃》的幕后故事
主作者
waychane
少数派成员
Finery fanatic.
waychane
waychane
少数派成员
Finery fanatic.
联合作者
waychane
少数派成员
Finery fanatic.
waychane
waychane
少数派成员
Finery fanatic.
10:27

《燃比娃》是由李文愉执导、上海电影(集团)有限公司及上海美术电影制片厂有限公司出品的动画电影，也是国内首部宣纸手绘动画长片。

本片导演李文愉毕业于北京大学、硕士研究生学历，毕业后任教于四川大学艺术学院数字艺术系，现担任四川大学艺术学院数字艺术系副教授。

2015年，李文愉执导的动画短片《Go To City ELE》就曾入选第 31 届华沙国际电影节短片竞赛单元，并荣获最佳动画短片奖；去年在柏林国际电影节新生代 Kplus 竞赛单元完成全球首映的《燃比娃》则是上海美术电影制片厂在《三个和尚》(1982) 和《鹬蚌相争》(1984) 之后，时隔 40 多年再度入围柏林国际电影节；此后也拿到过 2025 年 FIRST 青年电影展主竞赛评委会大奖、2025 年保加利亚瓦尔纳世界动画电影节 (WFAF) 最佳影片等多个国内外奖项。

《燃比娃》已于 2026 年 4 月 28 日登陆全国艺联专线，上映之前，我们有机会参与了电影的点映活动，提前观看本片并与导演李文愉进行了一次简单的交流，听他讲述了如何用 iPad 与 Apple Pencil 完成影片的创意工作。

用数字媒介重新诠释传统工艺

《燃比娃》的故事灵感源自羌族经典神话故事《燃比娃盗火》，羌族居民主要聚居在四川省阿坝藏族羌族自治州及绵阳市的北川羌族自治县，被誉为「云朵上的民族」，也被认为是中国古代西部游牧部落的后裔，与华夏族的形成密切相关。

本片讲述了主人公燃比娃为了拯救生活在冰天雪地中的人类，与名为「狗狗」的小狼为伴，共同克服恐惧、战胜怪兽，最终盗取火种的故事，燃比娃也在影片结尾揭开了身世真相，最终涅槃成人。

李文愉导演告诉我们，本片画面几乎全部以宣纸手绘的形式完成。对一部标准时长的院线长片而言，这样的选择意味着极其庞大的手绘工作量；但也恰恰是手绘宣纸画中水墨晕染、笔墨浓淡的随机性这些难以被工业化复制的细节，为影片打下了整体艺术风格和手工质感的重要基础。

影片中还特别加入了一段由四川省汶川县民间传统美术、国家级非物质文化遗产之一「羌绣」组成的定格动画。在被问及为什么要加入这样一段画面时，导演表示因为《燃比娃》是一个源自羌族的故事，而羌绣又是羌族最具有代表性的非物质文化遗产之一，无论是艺术性还是工艺本身都非常突出，一开始就有将其放在片中的想法。

去年七月在阿坝的若尔盖草原为影片采风时，极为开阔的视野加上正值花期的视觉冲击，让导演瞬间有了灵感：用羌绣将自己在这片草原花海目睹到的视觉震撼，转化为给观众的视觉冲击，从而产生共情。

为了更准确地传达这段羌绣画面希望传递的视觉效果，导演在自己的 iPad 上先用 Procreate 画出了近 300 张大致纹样的设计草图，再交由具有相关色彩研究经验的合作伙伴同样使用 iPad，设计出符合传统配色的最终版本，并与当地有经验的羌绣制作工坊合作，制作出羌绣实物。

不过，羌绣毕竟是在布料上制作的手工艺品，在拍摄阶段很难做到每块布料都能严丝合缝拼接成完整的平面画面。剧组团队在影片的后期制作阶段会用 Mac 上的 Photoshop 一点点调整，才让这段生动的羌绣画面最终呈现给观众。

从纸笔到 iPad 的五年

李文愉导演早期的创作方式依然是以传统的纸本速写为主，偶尔会用到电脑手绘板。而在过去五年创作《燃比娃》的过程中，他在创作工具以及方式上，也随着 iPad 与创作工具的迭代，发生了巨大的变化。

首先，对于导演而言，很多视觉想法和故事创意并非只在工作室里诞生，iPad 灵活便携的特点使草图记录和初步推敲不再受空间限制，这也是李文愉导演能够从纸笔过渡到完全使用 iPad 完成前期灵感记录和管理的关键。

此外，像是面对《燃比娃》这类以纸本手绘为创作和拍摄主体的作品而言，从灵感诞生到最终成片之间，还有非常多属于「中间状态」的创作内容，其中涉及到了大量诸如概念设定、视觉尝试等需要反复调整和筛选的流程。对于这类还在「漂移中」的素材而言，像是 iPad 这样的工具便能提供更为灵活和高效的调整及管理能力。

李文愉导演也反复提到了 Procreate for iPad 的笔刷功能：《燃比娃》是一部宣纸手绘动画长片，对于「纸上观感」的要求很高，数字笔刷能否与真实宣纸上的笔触形成匹配，对整部影片的美学呈现有很关键的作用。iPad 上的 Procreate 应用在过去五年时间里也获得了包括笔刷功能在内的各种更新内容，让 iPad 还原纸上手绘效果的能力得到了更多验证；许多在 iPad 上完成的补充设定画稿，都能够不突兀地与影片制作自然衔接。

为了统一影片的造型标准，李文愉导演在自己的 iPad 上用 Procreate 完成了近 200 张的造型指导手册，确保色彩、线条及笔墨上的一致性。李文愉导演最后也坦言，能够有 Procreate 这样的工具在 iPad 上实现自己的创意，真的是一件很有乐趣的事情。接下来，李文愉导演也已经有了新的创作计划，在继续保留手绘质感和个人特色的前提下，会尝试探索将开发商的另一款工具 Procreate Dreams 融入到创作流程中的可能性，以更高效及灵活的方式推进新作品。

动画人的一帧

在《燃比娃》的影片画面中有一段时长约 9 秒的快速剪辑镜头，其中包含了 69 位艺术家专门为本片创作的 69 幅画稿，这便是由《燃比娃》剧组发起的「动画人的一帧」计划。

在影片宣传阶段，剧组特地邀请了 69 位插画艺术家，将 69 张原画线稿交给他们，让他们在保留原有角色动态与构图位置的前提下，以自己的个人风格进行重绘。大部分艺术家都选择了使用 Procreate 在 iPad 上完成了此次创作，最终这些艺术家的单帧作品被重新串联在一起，形成了影片中这支完整的动态短片。

如果你走进影院欣赏这部影片，就能观看到这段以艺术史上不同经典风格的画面快速切换的动态短片，表现了人类从匍匐到直立行走的演化过程，呼应着影片想讨论的文明起源主题。

值得一提的是，今年也是上海美术电影制片厂成立的第 69 年，这支汇聚了 69 位艺术家的短片，加上《燃比娃》这部作品本身，也算是在这个特殊的时间送出的最好的礼物。

2

author: 少数派编辑部
summary: Valve 新一代 Steam 手柄宣布发售，中国联通发布「联通魔方」自选服务等。<a href="https://sspai.com/post/109204" target="_blank">查看全
content:
周三29
派早报：乐道 L80 发布、一加 Ace 6 至尊版发布等
今天开始倒数「什么时候放假」！
早报速览
  1. 乐道 L80 发布0
  2. 一加 Ace 6 至尊版发布6
  3. Valve 新一代 Steam 手柄宣布发售4
  4. 中国联通发布「联通魔方」自选服务4
  5. Google 翻译上线「发音练习」功能2
  6. 不妨一看的简讯1
  7. 少数派的近期动态0
  8. 你可能错过的好文章0

乐道 L80 发布

4 月 28 日，乐道汽车在杭州发布乐道 L80，该车定位为「智能双舱大五座旗舰 SUV」，车长 5145mm，可选 Orin-X 纯视觉或「神玑芯片」加激光雷达两种智驾方案，提供六种车身颜色及四种内饰主题；车型主打装载空间与场景扩展，包含三人骑行装载、定制露营套装及三段式随形大床等原厂拓展配件。

乐道 L80 整车预售价 24.58 万元起，BaaS 电池租用版 15.98 万元起，将于 5 月 1 日开启试驾，5 月 15 日正式上市。来源

一加 Ace 6 至尊版发布

4 月 28 日，一加发布一加 Ace 6 至尊版，搭载天玑 9500 移动平台和自研「风驰游戏内核」，配备「冰河散热系统」；屏幕为京东方 BOE 联合定制 165 Hz 超高刷东方屏，户外全局亮度 1800 nit，支持 0.1 ms 灰阶响应、96% 首帧亮度比、3840 Hz 超高频 PWM 调光；触控采用「灵犀触控芯」，支持 4000 Hz 瞬时触控采样率、480 Hz 多指采样率，配备最高 500 Hz 可调采样率陀螺仪，整机支持 IP66、IP68、IP69、IP69K 防尘防水；搭载 8600 mAh「冰川电池」支持 120 W 超级闪充；影像方面还配备 5000 万像素 1/1.55 英寸大底主摄。一加 Ace 6 至尊版已全渠道开售，定价 3099 元起（12GB+256GB）。

一加也同期发布了「一加枪神游戏手柄」，采用「触按融合」方案，配备电竞级微动机械开关，零售价 449 元。购买手机加购可享 50 元优惠。来源

有没有可能是ai生成的图片，然后没微调
这个配图是认真的么？官方图么？我看半天没理解是啥意思，为了凸显卡出残影？
全部讨论 6

Valve 新一代 Steam 手柄宣布发售

Valve 于 4 月 28 日宣布，新一代 Steam 控制器将于北京时间 5 月 5 日凌晨一点开始发售，该手柄无需搭配 Steam Machine 即可使用，可兼容所有运行 Steam 的设备或运行 Steam Link 应用的设备，包括 PC、Mac、移动设备和 Steam Deck。配置方面，新一代 Steam 控制器常规控制按键、Steam 键、快捷访问键、4 个可分配的握持键、两个全尺寸 TMR 磁性摇杆（带电容式触控功能）以及 6 轴 IMU 陀螺仪，此外还搭载 4 个触感马达，包括两个触控板内置的 LRA 触感马达和把手内置的两个高输出 LRA 触感马达，两侧各配备一块带触感反馈的 34.5mm 方形触控板，具备压力敏感性，可配置点击强度，控制器手柄背面还带有 2 个电容式感应区域。

新一代 Steam 控制器支持 2.4GHz 无线连接（轮询率 4ms），配备 8.39Wh 锂电池，续航可达 35 小时以上，重量约为 292g，定价 99 美元。来源

PS5/Xbox 手柄全新都不到500。
上一代的命运如何
99刀价格快赶上Xbox精英手柄加全套配件哩

中国联通发布「联通魔方」自选服务

4 月 28 日中国联通发布「联通魔方」自选式通信服务，该服务以「拖拉拽」自选式体验为核心，支持高自由度组合，允许用户随意搭配服务组件，支持线上自主调整与灵活变更。具体资费方面，「联通魔方」自选式通信服务在数据流量上采用阶梯计价，用户每月使用的前 20GB 按照 1.5 元 / GB 计费，超过 20GB 至 50GB 的部分降至 1.2 元 / GB，当月使用流量在 50GB 以上时，资费为 1 元 / GB，语音通信方面则采用按量计费模式，国内语音统一按 0.1 元 / 分钟收费，短彩信则按 0.1 元 / 条收取。

自选式通信服务的月基础消费门槛为 39 元，中国联通承诺将完全按照实际用多少、花多少的原则进行扣费，确保用户永远不会出现传统套餐中的「超套」问题。来源

仔细算来也不如普通的流量套餐实惠啊，再说不满1GB的情况也算一次计费吗？
所以之前那种19元100G+的套餐是绝版了么？
每个月按50g算，前20g是30，后30g是36，这就66了。还没算通话、短信，便宜吗？
流量按GB收费么……
4
2
3

Google 翻译上线「发音练习」功能

4 月 28 日，Google 在 Google 翻译上线 20 周年之际，宣布为 Google Translate 带来发音练习工具，用户获取翻译结果后可通过练习按钮朗读内容，由 AI 分析语音并提供即时反馈，该功能以今年早些时候推出的 Gemini Understand and Ask 能力为基础，面向美国和印度地区的 Android 用户开放，支持英语、西班牙语和印地语。来源

没找到这个功能，难道得科学到印度去？
这个感觉不错
12

不妨一看的简讯

  • Engadget 援引 The Information 消息称，Google 已与美国国防部签署一项机密协议，允许其 AI 模型用于「任何合法政府用途」，包括在机密环境中处理敏感任务，协议条款约束 AI 模型不得用于国内大规模监控，但 Google 无权否决美国政府的合法行动决策，并需在政府要求下协助调整 AI 安全设置和过滤器。该协议签署前，超过 600 名 Google 员工曾致信 CEO Sundar Pichai，要求公司不要让 AI 技术进入机密军事项目；Google 回应称，其 API 访问仍受标准条款约束，相关合作属于支持国家安全的负责任行为。来源
  • 9to5Google 援引爆料人 Lanzuk 在 Naver 的消息称，三星正考虑为 Galaxy S27 系列采用新的后置摄像头模组设计，取代当前已经延续了三年的设计方案，而 Qi2 磁吸配件的兼容性是主要原因之一。同时下一代机型的相机规格也可能修订。该变化仍存在不确定性，爆料称「近期成本问题」可能导致这一「完整改版」被暂缓。来源
  • Google 的快速分享 AirDrop 支持现已扩展至 OPPO Find X9 Ultra 和 vivo X300 Ultra 国际版。来源
do not evil～ 不过，谷人希当年拿着这个 title 都做一大堆不干净的事情。

author: 程天冲
summary: 上一篇文章中，我大概分析了一下租房常见的一些户型以及对应的问题，并且对一些通用的改造方法和原则做了简单的阐述。从这一期开始，这个主题将正式进入分区域对症下药的模式：详细探讨不同区域的针对性改造。最近上
content:
上一篇文章中，我大概分析了一下租房常见的一些户型以及对应的问题，并且对一些通用的改造方法和原则做了简单的阐述。从这一期开始，这个主题将正式进入分区域对症下药的模式：详细探讨不同区域的针对性改造。最近上海一直持续在阴天和下雨的天气中，我对阳光的需求量直线上升，所以决定还是把家里最重要的光源——阳台作为起点。希望可以用充足的光照，开启我们或忙碌或休闲的一天。

注：这个系列在格局上仅对一居室和一室开间进行讨论。虽然户型有限，但很多方法和原则是通用或可学习的，读者可以根据自己的实际情况进行参考。

阳台的格局与功能

格局

比阳台的格局更重要的，是阳台的朝向。北方地区的房子一般都相对「规整」，讲究坐北朝南，而南方地区遇见各种朝向阳台的概率就要相对大一些。不过这年头大家对朝向的重视程度不同，在租房的时候完全可以按照自己的喜好和要求来做选择。

朝向会影响房间的进光量和阳台的格局，正如第一篇文章中说的那样，阳台对租房来说算是一个奢侈品。两居室一般都能标配阳台甚至是落地飘窗，但一居室就没有这么幸福，经常要面临没有阳台、只有一面窗，或阳台和卧室合并，以及开放式阳台。

在这里，我按照常见程度给不同的阳台格局进行排序，方便你根据自己的需求进行挑选。

封闭式阳台（常见程度由高到低）：

  • 半独立空间，与卧室或客厅连接在一起
  • 独立空间，与客厅连接在一起，有明确的门与室内空间隔离
  • 独立空间，与卧室连接在一起，有明确的门与室内空间隔离
  • 独立空间，与厨房或卫生间连接，有独立的门与室内空间隔离

开放式阳台（常见程度由高到低）：

  • 独立空间，与客厅或卧室连接在一起，外侧可能有护栏，也可能无遮挡
  • 独立空间，与厨房或卫生间连接，外侧可能有护栏，也可能无遮挡
  • 无阳台，仅有一面大窗，但窗户附近有较小的空白空间

功能

不同的阳台格局，最终决定的是我们对阳台不同的用法。在我眼中，阳台的功能主要可以分为以下几类：

  • 晾晒优先型：如果家中没有烘干设备，那阳台基本只能用来保证晾晒
  • 收纳补充型：不少房东会在阳台做收纳柜，或者杂物架，充分利用阳台的每一寸空间用于收纳
  • 休闲下午茶型：像很多居家博主一样，阳台成为了放松、阅读、喝茶的休闲角落
  • 混合型：什么活儿都想让阳台干，什么功能都得支持，这应该也是我们很多人的阳台

格局和功能是要适配的，这也是这篇文章要做的主要工作。我曾经进入一个「误区」：根据格局来决定我的阳台做什么。后来发现这么做的问题就是贪心不足蛇吞象，想把所有的需求都塞进阳台里。塞是没问题的，但也应该从需求的急切程度出发，来决定怎么塞。因此我建议你暂时先忘记格局，先思考一个：我对阳台的需求是什么？

租户对阳台的常见诉求

同样的，我们根据需求的常见程度来进行排序：

  • 晾晒：排第一应该没什么问题。烘干设施并不是每家都有，也不是我们想实现就一定有条件。所以阳台无论朝向如何面积多大，晾晒基本都是最主流的需求
  • 储物：家里东西堆不下，经常需要储物和收纳，那阳台作为家中出现频率相对较低又有一定面积的角落，自然就成为了储物的备选地，尤其是很多时候成为衣物和清洁用具收纳存储的空间。
  • 放松娱乐：每个人都想过的需求，想要阳台担任家里的图书角和咖啡馆，想把阳光和能发照片的休闲时间拼接起来
  • 绿植养护：对于爱养绿植的朋友，阳台作为家里见光最好的区域，很自然会成为放置绿植的首选。

以上是常见的需求，每个人都还会有很多个性化的选择。譬如有些人将阳台作为办公区，享受在阳光沐浴下办公的感觉；还有人喜欢在阳台上搞手作，因为一些机器对通风有很大要求（例如家用的 3D 打印机）等等。接下来，我们从需求出发，看不同结构的阳台应该如何让我们的需求快速且有效适配。

晾晒：主流但不用占据全部

晾晒这个最重要的需求虽然很通用，然而把晾晒这个需求放进不同格局的阳台中也会有不一样。

大空间：不需要占据独立空间的全部

如果你的阳台是一个与卧室或者客厅连接的独立空间或者半独立空间，那么大概率是长条形的格局。房东给你的晾晒条件可能是：升降衣架、悬顶衣杆或是洗烘套装，基本上直接利用现成的条件就好。唯一需要注意的事项就是：不让晾晒占据空间的全部，可以留出一些空间（主要集中在两侧，或者正面靠墙的下侧）来满足其他需求。很多情况下我们会错误地让晾晒占据全部长方形的空间，这其实造成了不小的空间浪费，两侧和下侧的空地完全可以用来储物或其他需求。

房东如果配备了洗烘套装，基本上不是放在卫生间就是放在了阳台的一侧，我们自己想要增加洗烘设备也一般都是选择在空间的左右两侧，因此在人工晾晒的同时，侧面依然可以预留出来作为其他的功能区。假如房东没有预留晾衣架和洗烘套装，自己购置放在地上的折叠晾衣架，大概率很少有折叠起来的机会。这时候就可以认真使用晾衣架两侧的空间，或者把晾衣架放在一侧，空出另一侧的空间留作他用，努力让阳台满足更多的需求。

小空间：优先考虑洗烘，一定避免阴干

我在深圳租来的房子，就是一个和厨房连接在一起的方形阳台，没有封闭，通风环境尚可，但是因为太过狭小，衣服稍微一多就没法晾晒。同样的，很多阳台可能就是卫生间本身，受条件限制只能兼具洗衣和晾晒的功能；或是阳台是比较小的方形格局，这些情况下，我真的非常建议你购入洗烘一体机来解决晾衣问题，干脆让阳台去完成别的需求。

在经济或者客观条件不满足（例如没有合适的上下水条件）的情况下，我强烈建议你在卧室或客厅开辟出一个可以放折叠衣架的区域来晾晒，无论如何要避免阴干的情况。阴干衣服会导致细菌、蚊虫滋生等很多对健康和生活不利的问题，一定要杜绝。

author: Charles Pulliam-Moore
summary: In the days since this year's White House Correspondents' Dinner was cut short when shots were fired
content:
In the days since this year's White House Correspondents' Dinner was cut short when shots were fired at the event, there has been a boom of conspiracy theory videos created by people who insist that the entire situation was a false flag operation. These kinds of theories are nothing new, but the way they're spreading now is a reflection of how reaction video culture is reshaping our social media landscape. And even though the initial chaos around the shooting has started to die down, content creators are still posting about what "really" happened.

There is still much we do not know about Cole Allen, the 31-year-old suspected shooter who a …

Read the full story at The Verge.

author: Elizabeth Lopatto
summary: Today the first witness was sworn in in Musk v. Altman: Elon Musk. I was surprised by how flat he se
content:
Today the first witness was sworn in in Musk v. Altman: Elon Musk. I was surprised by how flat he seemed.

This is not the first time I've seen Musk in court. During his defamation suit, he turned on the charm and the jury responded by finding him not guilty. Today he looked adrift and unprepared. The only times he showed real animation were when he was bragging about how much he'd done for OpenAI.

The direct examination is a way of telling a story through questions; it's important to make the narrative clear. For a suit that accuses Sam Altman of straying from OpenAI's mission, Musk spent a weird amount of time talking about himself, recou …

Read the full story at The Verge.

author: Emma Roth
summary: The US Department of Justice has once again indicted James Comey - this time, for an alleged threat
content:
The US Department of Justice has once again indicted James Comey - this time, for an alleged threat the former FBI director made toward President Donald Trump on Instagram, as reported earlier by CNN. In its indictment, filed in a North Carolina federal court on Tuesday, the DOJ cites the now-deleted image Comey posted to Instagram last May, which showed a seashell arrangement making up the numbers "8647."

The DOJ claims "a reasonable recipient who is familiar with the circumstances would interpret as a serious expression of an intent to do harm." The phrase "eighty-six" can be used as slang that means to "get rid of" or "throw out," accord …

Read the full story at The Verge.

author: Thomas Macaulay
summary: This is today’s edition of The Download, our weekday newsletter that provides a daily dose of
content:
This is today’s edition of The Download, our weekday newsletter that provides a daily dose of what’s going on in the world of technology.

Elon Musk and Sam Altman are going to court over OpenAI’s future

Elon Musk and OpenAI CEO Sam Altman head to trial this week in a case with sweeping consequences. Ahead of OpenAI’s IPO, the court could rule on whether the company can exist as a for-profit enterprise. It could even oust its leadership.

Musk, an OpenAI co-founder, claims he was deceived into bankrolling the firm under false pretenses. He’s seeking $134 billion in damages, the removal of Altman and president Greg Brockman, and the company’s restoration to a non-profit.

Find out how the trial could upend the global AI race.

—Michelle Kim

The missing step between hype and profit

In a celebrated South Park episode, a community of gnomes sneak out at night to steal underpants. Why? The gnomes present their pitch deck. “Phase 1: Collect underpants. Phase 2: ? Phase 3: Profit.” It’s a business plan that captures the current state of AI. 

Companies have built the tech (Step 1) and promised transformation (Step 3). But how they get there is still a big question mark. Read about the potential paths forward.


—Will Douglas Heaven

This story originally appeared in The Algorithm, our weekly newsletter giving you the inside track on all things AI. Sign up to receive it in your inbox every Monday.

Welcome to the era of weaponized deepfakes

For years, experts have warned that deepfakes could be deployed in malicious ways. These dangers are now here.

Cheap, accessible models now produce weaponized deepfakes—from sexually explicit images to political propaganda—that look startlingly real. They’re already inciting violence, changing minds, and sowing mistrust, with women and marginalized groups disproportionately affected.

Experts fear that they’re cratering trust and critical thinking. Here’s why they’re alarmed.

—Eileen Guo

Weaponized deepfakes are on our list of the 10 Things That Matter in AI Right Now, MIT Technology Review’s guide to what’s really worth your attention in the busy, buzzy world of AI. 

The must-reads

I’ve combed the internet to find you today’s most fun/important/scary/fascinating stories about technology.

1 OpenAI has ended its exclusive partnership with Microsoft
The new deal allows OpenAI to court rivals such as Amazon. (Reuters $)
+ Microsoft will still license OpenAI’s tech, but no longer exclusively. (NYT $)
+ OpenAI is missing key growth targets ahead of its IPO. (WSJ $)

2 Google has signed a classified AI deal with the Pentagon
It permits AI use for “any lawful government purpose.” (The Information $)
+ Over 600 Google workers had called for a block on the deal. (QZ)
+ AI firms are set to train military versions of their models on classified data. (MIT Technology Review)

3 The EU has told Google to open Android to AI rivals
It wants to end Gemini’s built-in advantage. (Ars Technica)
+ Google calls the move an “unwarranted intervention.” (WSJ $)
+ A final decision is expected by the end of July. (Reuters $)

4 OpenAI is reportedly developing an AI-first smartphone
It would replace apps with agents. (TechCrunch)
+ Qualcomm and MediaTek may be developing its processors. (Gizmodo)

5 A brain implant for depression is moving into human testing
The FDA has approved a human study of the device. (Wired $)
+ BCIs have thus far struggled to reach the market. (MIT Technology Review)

6 A populist backlash against AI is gaining momentum in rural America
From Indiana to Idaho, voters are pushing back against the technology. (NYT $)
+ Anti-AI protests are expanding worldwide. (MIT Technology Review)

7 DeepSeek has priced its new model 97% below OpenAI’s GPT-5.5
It aims to attract more enterprises, developers, and agent-based users. (SCMP)
+ Here are three reasons why DeepSeek V4 matters. (MIT Technology Review)

8 AI now generates a third of new websites
A study found it’s making the web more cheery and less verbose. (404 Media)

9 Top talent is leaving Big Tech to launch their own AI startups
Meta, Google, and OpenAI are facing a brain drain. (CNBC)

10 Taylor Swift is trademarking her voice and image
The Grammy winner has been the target of numerous deepfakes. (NBC News)
+ A growing number of celebrities are fighting AI with trademarks. (BBC)

Quote of the day

“The reality is people don’t like him.”

—Judge Yvonne Gonzalez Rogers reacts to prospective jurors confessing their negative views of Elon Musk ahead of his legal battle with Sam Altman, The Verge reports.

One More Thing


How covid conspiracy theories led to an alarming resurgence in AIDS denialism

When Joe Rogan falsely declared that “party drugs” were an “important factor in AIDS,” several million people were listening. He also asserted that AZT, the earliest drug used to treat AIDS, killed people “quicker” than the disease itself—another claim that has been disproven.

Such comments illustrate an unmistakable resurgence in AIDS denialism: a false collection of theories arguing either that HIV does not cause AIDS or that there is no such thing as HIV at all. By the dawn of the millennium, these claims had largely fallen out of favour. That changed when the coronavirus arrived.

Follow the digital path from Covid skepticism to the return of a deadly conspiracy theory.


—Anna Merlan

We can still have nice things

A place for comfort, fun and distraction to brighten up your day. (Got any ideas? Drop me a line.)

+ Explore the planets from your laptop with this live sky map.
+ This marathon DJ set from Daphni is an incredible journey through electronic music.
+ NASA’s stunning Artemis II wallpapers bring a high-res piece of deep space to your phone.
+ This fascinating GPS explainer breaks down how your phone figures out exactly where you are.

author: Michelle Kim
summary: After a yearslong legal feud, Elon Musk and OpenAI CEO Sam Altman are heading to trial this week in
content:
After a yearslong legal feud, Elon Musk and OpenAI CEO Sam Altman are heading to trial this week in Northern California in a case that could have sweeping consequences. Ahead of OpenAI’s highly anticipated IPO, the court could rule on whether the company is allowed to exist as a for-profit enterprise and might even oust its current executive leadership, including Altman.

Musk is suing OpenAI, alleging that Altman and OpenAI president Greg Brockman deceived him into bankrolling the company in its early days by promising to maintain it as a nonprofit dedicated to developing AI that benefits humanity, only to later restructure the company to operate a for-profit subsidiary. Musk cofounded OpenAI with Altman and others in 2015, but he left in 2018 after a bitter power struggle. 

Musk is seeking as much as $134 billion in damages from OpenAI and Microsoft, one of OpenAI’s biggest financial backers. He is also asking the court to remove Altman and Brockman from their roles and to restore OpenAI as a nonprofit. Musk has asked the court to award any damages to OpenAI’s nonprofit rather than to him personally. 

Nine jurors will deliver an advisory verdict, a non-binding recommendation, to guide the judge in deciding Musk’s claims against Altman. Musk, Altman, and Brockman will take the stand. Former OpenAI chief scientist Ilya Sutskever, former OpenAI CTO Mira Murati, and Microsoft CEO Satya Nadella are also expected to testify. Cringey texts, raw diary entries, and endless scheming behind the founding and growth of OpenAI are expected to come to light.

In an industry enveloped in secrecy, the trial will be a rare opportunity for the public to look behind the curtain and find out what’s going on in the companies creating the most transformative technology ever built. 

What are they fighting about?

When OpenAI was originally founded as a nonprofit, backed by a $38 million donation from Musk, the company vowed to create open-source technology for the public’s benefit, unconstrained by a need to generate financial returns. But over the years, the company began to believe that intensifying competition could make it dangerous to share how it develops its AI models and that a nonprofit structure could not raise enough money to keep building AI. (MIT Technology Review was first to report on OpenAI’s internal conflicts around its mission.)

The court has already found that in 2017 Altman and Brockman wanted to establish a for-profit arm, while Musk proposed merging OpenAI with his electric-car company, Tesla. When Musk threatened to stop funding, Altman and Brockman told him that they were committed to keeping the company a nonprofit. Musk alleges that they pursued plans to pivot to a for-profit without sufficiently informing him. According to OpenAI, Musk agreed that the company needed a for-profit entity and even wanted to be its CEO. 

But even if Musk proves he was duped by Altman and Brockman, he may not have standing in the first place to sue them for restructuring the company to operate a for-profit subsidiary. Some legal scholars are puzzled over why the judge allowed him to bring this claim. “The idea that Elon Musk can sue because he was a donor or used to be on the board is pretty puzzling,” says Jill Horwitz, a law professor who studies nonprofit law at Northwestern University. “Typically, it’s up to the attorneys general to bring such a claim to enforce the charitable purposes. And that’s already happened.” 

In October 2025, state attorneys general of California, where OpenAI is headquartered, and Delaware, where OpenAI is incorporated, struck a deal with OpenAI to approve its new corporate structure on a series of conditions. For example, a safety and security committee at the nonprofit would review safety-related decisions made by the for-profit subsidiary. Critics of the restructuring, including Musk, AI safety advocates, and civil society groups, have tried to stop it. 

California’s attorney general has declined to join Musk’s lawsuit, saying that the office did not see how his action serves the public interest.

Still, whether the deals hold OpenAI to its nonprofit mission is an open question. “Elon Musk should have to show … what the deficiencies are in what’s been agreed to by OpenAI with the attorneys general,” says Rose Chan Loui, the director of the UCLA School of Law’s philanthropy and nonprofit program. Even with the terms in place, holding OpenAI to them depends on “how much they can enforce it and how much transparency they get into OpenAI’s work.”

More importantly, legal experts say the case is being considered under the wrong body of law. Musk argues that Altman and Brockman breached OpenAI’s charitable trust by creating a closed-source, for-profit subsidiary. As a result, the court has been analyzing the claim under the law of trusts. “But OpenAI is not a trust. OpenAI is a corporation. And so really they should be looking at … the law of charitable nonprofit organizations,” says Chan Loui.

What’s on the line?

Despite all the legal muddiness, the outcome of the trial could upend the AI race. Any one of the remedies that Musk seeks could cripple OpenAI as it races to go public by the end of the year. OpenAI, which is valued at over $850 billion, has described the litigation with Musk as a potential risk to its business. Musk’s rival company xAI, which makes the chatbot Grok, is expected to go public as a part of his rocket company SpaceX as early as June. If Musk prevails, xAI, which in combination with SpaceX is valued at $1.25 trillion, could get a big advantage in the AI race. 

And the trial has helped expose the bitter schism between Musk and the company he once helped to found. An OpenAI spokesperson referred MIT Technology Review to a post on X: “This lawsuit has always been a baseless and jealous bid to derail a competitor.” Although Musk’s lawyers did not immediately respond to a request for comment, he has posted on X that “Scam Altman lies as easily as he breathes.”  

MIT Technology Review will have ongoing coverage of Musk v. Altman until its conclusion. Follow @techreview or @michelletomkim on X or @michelletomkim on Bluesky for up-to-the-minute reporting.

author: Will Douglas Heaven
summary: This story originally appeared in The Algorithm, our weekly newsletter on AI. To get stories like th
content:
This story originally appeared in The Algorithm, our weekly newsletter on AI. To get stories like this in your inbox first, sign up here.

In February, I picked up a flyer at an anti-AI march in London. I can’t say for sure whether or not its writers meant to riff on South Park’s underpants gnomes. But if they did, they nailed it: “Step 1: Grow a digital super mind,” it read. “Step 2: ? Step 3: ?”

Produced by Pause AI, an international activist group that co-organized the protest, it ended with this plea to the reader: “Pause AI until we know what the hell Step 2 is.” 

In the South Park episode “Gnomes,” which first aired in 1998, Kenny, Kyle, Cartman, and Stan discover a community of gnomes that sneak out at night to steal underpants from dressers. Why? The gnomes present their pitch deck. “Phase 1: Collect underpants. Phase 2: ? Phase 3: Profit.”

The gnomes’ business plan has since become one of the greats among internet memes, used to satirize everything from startup strategies to policy proposals. Memelord in chief Elon Musk once invoked it in a talk about how he planned to fund a mission to Mars. Right now, it captures the state of AI. Companies have built the tech (Step 1) and promised transformation (Step 3). How they get there is still a big question mark.

As far as Pause AI is concerned, Step 2 must involve some kind of regulation. But exactly what it will call for and who will enforce it are up for debate.

AI boosters, on the other hand, are convinced that Step 3 is salvation and tend to glaze over the middle bit. They see us racing toward sunny uplands on the back of an “economically transformative technology,” as OpenAI’s chief scientist, Jakub Pachocki, put it to me a few weeks ago. They know where they want to go—more or less: It’s hazy up there and still some way off. But everyone’s taking a different route. Will they all make it? Will anyone?

For every big claim about the future, there is a more sober assessment of how the rubber meets the road—one that quells the hype. Consider two recent studies. One, from Anthropic, predicted what types of jobs are going to be most affected by LLMs. (A takeaway: Managers, architects, and people in the media should prepare for change; groundskeepers, construction workers, and those in hospitality, not so much.) But their predictions are really just guesses, based on what kinds of tasks LLMs seem to be good at rather than how they really perform in the workplace.   

Another study, put out in February by researchers at Mercor, an AI hiring startup, tested several AI agents powered by top-tier models from OpenAI, Anthropic, and Google DeepMind on 480 workplace tasks frequently carried out by human bankers, consultants, and lawyers. Every agent they tested failed to complete most of its duties.   

Why is there such wide disagreement? There are a number of factors. For a start, it’s crucial to consider who is making the claims (and why). Anthropic has skin in the game. What’s more, most of the people telling us that something big is about to happen have reached that conclusion largely on the basis of how fast AI coding tools are getting. But not all tasks can be hacked with coding. Other studies have found that LLMs are bad at making strategic judgment calls, for example.

What’s more, when they’re deployed, the tools aren’t just dropped into a cleanroom. They need to work in places contaminated with people and existing workflows. And sometimes adding AI will make things worse. Sure, maybe those workflows need to be torn up and refashioned around the new technology for it to achieve transformative status, but that will take time (and guts).  

That big hole? It’s right where Step 2 should be. The lack of agreement on exactly what’s about to happen—and how—creates an information vacuum that gets filled by the latest wild claim of the week, evidence be damned. We’re so unmoored from any real understanding of what’s coming and how it will be deployed that a single social media post can (and does) shake markets.

We need fewer guesses and more evidence. But that’s going to require transparency from the model makers, coordination between researchers and businesses, and new ways to evaluate this technology that tell us what really happens when it’s rolled out in the real world.

The tech industry (and with it the world’s economy) rests on the held-out promise that AI really will be transformative. But that is not yet a sure bet. Next time you hear bold claims about the future, remember that most businesses are still figuring out what to do with their underpants.

In Brief

Posted:

5:40 PM PDT · April 28, 2026
  • Connie Loizos

At his OpenAI trial, Musk relitigates an old friendship

Among the most interesting parts of Elon Musk’s testimony Tuesday in his lawsuit against OpenAI wasn’t the charity he claims was stolen from him (we all knew that was coming). It was about an old friend.

Musk testified that one of his core motivations for co-founding OpenAI was a falling-out with Google’s Larry Page over AI safety — specifically, a conversation in which Musk raised the prospect of AI wiping out humanity and Page shrugged it off as “fine,” so long as AI itself survived. Page called Musk a “speciest” for being “pro human.” Musk called the attitude “insane.”

That’s mostly notable given how close the two once were. Fortune included them on its 2016 list of secretly best-friend business leaders; Musk was so comfortable with Page that he regularly crashed at his Palo Alto home. Page once told Charlie Rose that he’d rather give his money to Musk than to charity.

The friendship didn’t survive OpenAI. When Musk recruited Google AI star Ilya Sutskever to help launch the company in 2015, Page felt personally betrayed and cut off contact.

It’s a story Musk has told before — including to author Walter Isaacson for his bestselling biography of Musk — but Tuesday was the first time he said it under oath. Page hasn’t commented, and it’s worth remembering everything that Musk said was in service of a lawsuit. Still, as recently as 2023 he told tech podcaster Lex Fridman he wanted to patch things up: “We were friends for a very long time.”

Topics

AI, Elon Musk, Google, In Brief, Larry Page, OpenAI

In Brief

Posted:

12:48 PM PDT · April 28, 2026
  • Julie Bort

Amazon is already offering new OpenAI products on AWS

Almost as soon as OpenAI announced that its major investor and cloud partner, Microsoft, no longer has exclusive rights to any of its products, Amazon started gloating.

After the revised OpenAI/Microsoft agreement was announced on Monday, Amazon CEO Andy Jassy noted in a tweet that it was a “very interesting announcement.” That agreement solved OpenAI’s problem of allowing AWS to offer its products, an issue that crystalized after it signed an up-to-$50-billion deal with Amazon.

Amazon announced on Tuesday that AWS’s Bedrock service now has OpenAI’s latest models, its code-writing service Codex, and a new product for creating OpenAI-powered AI agents. Bedrock is Amazon’s AI app building and model-choosing service.

Amazon is calling the new agent service Bedrock Managed Agents. It is specifically designed to use OpenAI’s reasoning models, offering features like agent steering and security.

Amazon promises in its blog post that “this is the beginning of a deeper collaboration between AWS and OpenAI.” And it will certainly be interesting to watch.

The Microsoft/OpenAI relationship has reportedly been deteriorating for some time, with each of them finding comfort in the arms of their partner’s biggest rival. OpenAI has turned to AWS and Oracle. Microsoft to Anthropic; the Redmond-based software giant is also working on a new agent offering powered by Claude.

AI

Amazon launches an AI-powered audio Q&A experience on product pages

Lauren Forristal
11:49 AM PDT · April 28, 2026

Amazon launched a new AI-powered feature on Tuesday that allows users to ask questions about products and receive conversational audio responses generated in real time. The responses are delivered by what the company calls “AI-powered shopping experts,” which present information in a natural, discussion-style format.

The new “Join the chat” feature aims to save customers time by providing key product details without requiring them to scroll through lengthy descriptions or reviews. The AI pulls together insights about product features, customer feedback, and other relevant information. For example, shoppers can ask questions like whether a coffee maker is suited for beginners or whether a sweater feels itchy based on customer reviews.

Rather than giving generic answers, Amazon says the AI builds on previous responses to provide more relevant and helpful information, while also making sure not to repeat anything. This is meant to be a similar experience to speaking with a knowledgeable employee at a store.  

“Customers can ask questions and actually steer where the conversation goes. Every question they ask influences what comes next, making the experience a conversation customers can join and customize,” the company writes in a blog post. 

“Join the chat” is part of a broader experience called “Hear the highlights,” which offers short audio summaries on millions of product pages within the Amazon Shopping app. That feature began testing last May and is currently available in the U.S. However, only select products have audio summaries.

To use the feature, customers open a product page in the app and tap the “Hear the highlights” button, located below the product image. From there, they can listen to a brief overview or tap the “Join the chat” icon to ask specific questions via text or voice. The audio can continue playing even as users browse.

The new capability builds on Amazon’s growing lineup of AI-driven shopping tools. These include Rufus, its generative AI assistant that helps customers research products and compare options; Interests, which continuously tracks and surfaces new items aligned with a shopper’s preferences; and “Help me decide,” which suggests products based on a person’s searches, browsing, and shopping history.

Topics

AI, Amazon, Apps, Commerce, eCommerce, Rufus
